conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules, then I'll ask you some questions about your situation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I have reviewed the eligibility rules. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have two children. Alex is 4 years old and Blake is 14.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have two children - Alex (age 4) and Blake (age 14).

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. Do Alex and Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to ask: do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep? This could include things like paying for food, clothes, pocket money, or contributing to their accommodation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with Alex's upkeep. I contribute towards things like clothes, food, and other bits and pieces they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Can you tell me approximately how much you contribute towards Alex's upkeep each week or month? I need to check if it's at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount (which is currently around £25 per week for the eldest child and around £16.95 for additional children).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful information. Now I just need to check a few more details:

Are either Alex or Blake currently in hospital, care, or residential accommodation for an extended period?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in hospital, care, or residential accommodation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. One final question:

Has Blake (your 14-year-old) started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or are they receiving any benefits in their own right such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work that many hours and isn't receiving any benefits in their own right.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Based on the information you've provided, let me evaluate your situation for each child:

**For Blake (14 years old):**
- Blake lives with you in the UK
- Blake is under 16 years old
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake
- Blake is not working 24+ hours per week or receiving benefits in their own right
- You are clearly responsible for Blake

**For Alex (4 years old):**
- Alex doesn't live with you, but you contribute £50 per week towards their upkeep
- This is well above the Child Benefit amount (approximately £25 per week for an eldest child or £16.95 for an additional child)
- According to the rules, you're considered responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

You meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for both children.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.**

You can proceed to apply for Child Benefit. Remember that only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child, so if someone else later wants to claim for either Alex or Blake, you would need to contact the Child Benefit Office to sort that out.

Please note: If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but you can still claim the benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old (under 16). Although Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount. According to the eligibility rules, a person is normally responsible for a child if they are paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (e.g., on food, clothes, or pocket money). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant lives in the UK. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 14 years old (under 16) and lives with the claimant in the UK. The claimant is clearly responsible for Blake. Blake is not working 24+ hours per week or receiving benefits in their own right. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Blake is not in hospital, care, or residential accommodation. Therefore, the claimant meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has two children: Alex (age 4) and Blake (age 14). Blake lives with the claimant, making them clearly responsible. Alex does not live with the claimant, but the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount and establishes responsibility under the rules. Neither child is in hospital, care, or residential accommodation, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child. Both children are under 16. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Therefore, the claimant meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Blake.
